description: this rule identify attempts of exploitation towards atlassian vulnerabilities, specifically cve-2023-22518. this vulnerability involves an improper authorization flaw that, when exploited, enables a remote attacker to gain unauthorized access. exploitation of this vulnerability aims to facilitate initial access to victims' environments, followed by the deployment of a java-based web shell. subsequently, this may lead to the deployment of conti or cerber ransomware payloads. this rule looks for the http requests that was observed in the exploitation of this vulnerability. on triggering of this rule, analysts are recommended to verify the network traffic observed. more information on this vulnerability can be found in the infolinks given.

alert_type_details: {"detail": {"msg": "creating scriptblock text (1 of 1):  function invoke-webrequestverifyhash ($url, $outfile, $hash) {     $success = $false     $null = @(         new-item -itemtype directory (split-path $outfile) -force | out-null         $ms = new-object io.memorystream         [net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol = ([net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls11 -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls12)         (new-object system.net.webclient).openread($url).copyto($ms)         $ms.seek(0, [system.io.seekorigin]::begin) | out-null         $actualhash = (get-filehash -inputstream $ms).hash         if ( $hash -eq $actualhash) {             $ms.seek(0, [system.io.seekorigin]::begin) | out-null             $filestream = new-object io.filestream $outfile, ([system.io.filemode]::create)             $ms.copyto($filestream);             $filestream.close()             $success = $true         }         else {             write-host -foregroundcolor red \"file hash mismatch, expected: $hash, actual: $actualhash\"         }     )     $success }     scriptblock id: 65955529-9229-447d-97e1-a16dbdfabb73  path: c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1", "pid": 30552, "class": "ms_windows_event", "eventid": "4104", "version": "10.0.22631", "category": "execute a remote command", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "hostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "severity": "warning", "threadid": "31648", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "metaclass": "windows", "accountname": "us58432", "application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1", "accountdomain": "corp", "rawmsghostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org"}, "source": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "summary": {"application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1"}, "properties": {"class": "ms_windows_event", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "customer_id": "hexbdw276"}, "destination": "4104"}
description: this rule looks for a powershell command that includes the .net webclient class or any indication of a url. attackers can use this method when using powershell as a downloader for additional payload. this method could also be used for legitimate reasons. if this rule triggers an alert, inspect the url to be downloaded for evidence of malice. tune this rule by filtering on the "application", "filename", or "filepath" fields.

description: this rule detects the certutil windows built-in command being executed with a suspicious encoding argument. certutil can be utilized to encode stolen information in a privacy enhanced mail (pem) certificate and exfiltrate it to the c2 web application, making traffic detection more difficult. kimsuky has previously employed a technique for exfiltrating data hidden inside pem files.

account control policy.    type 1 is a full token with no privileges removed or groups disabled.  a full token is only used if user account control is disabled or if the user is the built-in privilege, and the user is a member of the administrators group.    type 3 is a limited token with administrative privileges removed and administrative groups disabled.  the limited token is used when description: this rule looks for multiple instances of taskkill.exe being called by the same user on a host. while this can be legitimate administrative activity, it could also be used by attackers to kill things such as monitoring or logging processes to hide malicious activity. if this rule triggers an alert, examine the alert to determine what processes were being killed and if this is benign activity.

description: this ioc covers powershell command line arguments commonly used by attackers to hide activity and avoid dropping malware to disk.   these commands could be used by administrators for legitimate operations, but are more often observed in malicious operations.  if there is no known authorized use in your environment, investigate the source endpoint and any endpoints named in the command line arguments for further evidence.  malicious command line arguments will often be heavily obfuscated or encoded. this is associated with mitre att&ck (r) tactic(s): execution and technique(s): t1059.001.
message: trellix endpoint hx [ioc alert - suspicious powershell usage (methodology)]

description: this rule looks for the initiation of a windows scheduled task from the command line. attackers use this feature as a persistence mechanism, or to invoke a malicious job or script at a given time. these tasks are often named as simply as 1.exe or as innocuously as defrag.bat. determine whether the scheduled task contains malicious activity.
message: windows methodology [scheduled task initiation from cli]

description: a malware callback alert detects network activity that matches a malware command and control (c2) signature. this should only fire when a host is already compromised. the recommended action is to investigate the host and possibly remove it from the network. this activity will be blocked if the nx is in block mode.

description: this rule looks for multiple office 365 "secure links" being created or used in a short period of time by one user. secure links are a mechanism that allows files to be securely shared outside of an organization with various permissions that can be applied. secure links could potentially be used to exfiltrate data outside of an organization by masking the transfer as a legitimate link share. if this rule triggers an alert, examine the alert to see if the content and recipient of the link are legitimate.

description: this rule looks for the creation of a windows scheduled task from the command line. attackers use this feature as a persistence mechanism or to invoke a malicious job or script at a given time. these tasks are often named as simply as 1.exe or as innocuously as defrag.bat. if this rule triggers an alert, check whether the scheduled task contains malicious activity.
message: windows methodology [scheduled task creation from cli]

description: a malware callback alert detects network activity that matches a malware command and control (c2) signature. this should only fire when a host is already compromised. the recommended action is to investigate the host and possibly remove it from the network. this activity will be blocked if the nx is in block mode.

description: this rule looks for suspicious rundll32.exe process executions with command lines that invoke control_rundll or shellexec_rundll functions from shell32.dll, a core windows shell dll. attackers leverage this legitimate functionality as a living off the land binary (lolbin) to execute arbitrary code, launch dll payloads, executables, or even command-line arguments, serving as a versatile method for code execution and defense evasion.
message: windows methodology [rundll32 - shell32.dll arbitrary code execution]

subject:   security id:  s-1-0-0   account name:  -   account domain:  -   logon id:  0x0    process information:   new process id:  0x1508   new process name: c:\\windows\\system32\\netsh.exe   token elevation type: tokenelevationtypedefault (1)   mandatory label:  s-1-16-16384   creator process id: 0x38d4   creator process name: c:\\windows\\system32\\netsh.exe   process command line: \"c:\\windows\\system32\\netsh.exe\" lan show profile wi-fi    token elevation type indicates the type of token that was assigned to the new process in accordance with user account control policy.    type 1 is a full token with no privileges removed or groups disabled.  a full token is only used if user account control is disabled or if the user is the built-in administrator account or a service account.    type 2 is an elevated token with no privileges removed or groups disabled.  an elevated token is used when user account control is enabled and the user chooses to start the program using run as administrator.  an elevated token is also used when an application is configured to always require administrative privilege or to always require maximum privilege, and the user is a member of the administrators group.    type 3 is a limited token with administrative privileges removed and administrative groups disabled.  the limited token is used when user account control is enabled, the application does not require administrative privilege, and the user does not choose to start the program using run as administrator.", "pid": 5384, "args": "\"C:\\WINDOWS\\system32\\netsh.exe\" lan description: this rule looks for netsh (a built-in windows process) as a parent process. this is likely not a typical event and could indicate that netsh was used to persist malware on a host. click the attached link for more information.

description: this rule looks for a windows event showing an instance of winlogon.exe (windows logon process) whose parent process is not smss.exe (session manager). this should never occur during normal operation and can indicate a process that is impersonating winlogon.exe or other malicious activity. the winlogon process handles user logon and logoff.

description: a domain match event detects when a client has requested dns to resolve a known malicious hostname. this alert just means that dns was resolved. it does not indicate that any malicious payload was downloaded. since this alert does not confirm a compromise, it will take additional work to determine the level of response needed. several hits from the same source to unique hostnames may be a better indicator of a compromised machine. this activity will not be blocked if the nx is in block mode.

description: this rule looks for the creation of a windows scheduled task from the command line. attackers use this feature as a persistence mechanism or to invoke a malicious job or script at a given time. these tasks are often named as simply as 1.exe or as innocuously as defrag.bat. if this rule triggers an alert, check whether the scheduled task contains malicious activity.
message: windows methodology [scheduled task creation from cli]

description: this rule looks for suspicious rundll32.exe process executions with command lines that invoke control_rundll or shellexec_rundll functions from shell32.dll, a core windows shell dll. attackers leverage this legitimate functionality as a living off the land binary (lolbin) to execute arbitrary code, launch dll payloads, executables, or even command-line arguments, serving as a versatile method for code execution and defense evasion.
message: windows methodology [rundll32 - shell32.dll arbitrary code execution]

description: this rule looks for suspicious rundll32.exe process executions attempting to open urls. it specifically targets command lines invoking openurl or fileprotocolhandler functions from dlls like url.dll, ieframe.dll, or shdocvw.dll. attackers leverage this technique to achieve code execution, data theft, or phishing attempts on a compromised system.

description: this rule looks for the creation of a windows scheduled task from the command line. attackers use this feature as a persistence mechanism or to invoke a malicious job or script at a given time. these tasks are often named as simply as 1.exe or as innocuously as defrag.bat. if this rule triggers an alert, check whether the scheduled task contains malicious activity.
message: windows methodology [scheduled task creation from cli]

alert_type_details: {"detail": {"msg": "creating scriptblock text (1 of 1):  function invoke-fetchfromzip {     param(         [parameter(mandatory = $true, position = 0)]         [string]         $zipurl,         [parameter(mandatory = $true, position = 1)]         [string]         $targetfilter, # files that match this filter will be copied to the destinationpath, retaining their folder path from the zip         [parameter(mandatory = $true, position = 2)]         [string]         $destinationpath     )      # load zip methods     add-type -assemblyname system.io.compression.filesystem     [system.reflection.assembly]::loadwithpartialname('system.io.compression') | out-null      # read zip archive into memory     $ms = new-object io.memorystream     [net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol = ([net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls11 -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls12)     (new-object system.net.webclient).openread($zipurl).copyto($ms)     $zip = new-object system.io.compression.ziparchive($ms)      # ensure the output folder exists     $parent = split-path $destinationpath     $exists = test-path -path $parent     if ($exists -eq $false) {         $null = new-item -path $destinationpath -itemtype directory -force     }      # find all files in zip that match the filter (i.e. file extension)     $zip.entries |     where-object {             ($_.fullname -like $targetfilter)     } |     foreach-object {         # extract the selected items from the zip archive         # and copy them to the out folder         $dstdir = join-path $destinationpath ($_.fullname | split-path | split-path -leaf)         new-item -itemtype directory -force -path $dstdir | out-null         [system.io.compression.zipfileextensions]::extracttofile($_, (join-path $dstdir $_.name), $true)     }     $zip.dispose() }     scriptblock id: 018a0f6d-f08c-4b7e-9886-ab6811d36d05  path: c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-fetchfromzip.ps1", "pid": 28728, "class": "ms_windows_event", "eventid": "4104", "version": "10.0.22631", "category": "execute a remote command", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "hostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "severity": "warning", "threadid": "14944", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "metaclass": "windows", "accountname": "us58432", "application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-fetchfromzip.ps1", "accountdomain": "corp", "rawmsghostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org"}, "source": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "summary": {"application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-fetchfromzip.ps1"}, "properties": {"class": "ms_windows_event", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "customer_id": "hexbdw276"}, "destination": "4104"}

description: powershell is a scripting language and command-line shell that is bundled with all modern versions of windows. while this tool is used for all kinds of legitimate purposes, attackers also leverage it to compromise windows systems. additionally, attackers frequently use obfuscation techniques in an attempt to bypass detection mechanisms. however, if obfuscation is detected, it can be a telltale sign of malicious intent. this rule detects a powershell argument that has high number of dollar sign characters, indicating many variables were set. this is likely an unusual thing to see on the command line and could be an indicator of string obfuscation.

description: a domain match event detects when a client has requested dns to resolve a known malicious hostname. this alert just means that dns was resolved. it does not indicate that any malicious payload was downloaded. since this alert does not confirm a compromise, it will take additional work to determine the level of response needed. several hits from the same source to unique hostnames may be a better indicator of a compromised machine. this activity will not be blocked if the nx is in block mode.

alert_type_details: {"detail": {"msg": "creating scriptblock text (1 of 1):  function invoke-fetchfromzip {     param(         [parameter(mandatory = $true, position = 0)]         [string]         $zipurl,         [parameter(mandatory = $true, position = 1)]         [string]         $targetfilter, # files that match this filter will be copied to the destinationpath, retaining their folder path from the zip         [parameter(mandatory = $true, position = 2)]         [string]         $destinationpath     )      # load zip methods     add-type -assemblyname system.io.compression.filesystem     [system.reflection.assembly]::loadwithpartialname('system.io.compression') | out-null      # read zip archive into memory     $ms = new-object io.memorystream     [net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol = ([net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls11 -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls12)     (new-object system.net.webclient).openread($zipurl).copyto($ms)     $zip = new-object system.io.compression.ziparchive($ms)      # ensure the output folder exists     $parent = split-path $destinationpath     $exists = test-path -path $parent     if ($exists -eq $false) {         $null = new-item -path $destinationpath -itemtype directory -force     }      # find all files in zip that match the filter (i.e. file extension)     $zip.entries |     where-object {             ($_.fullname -like $targetfilter)     } |     foreach-object {         # extract the selected items from the zip archive         # and copy them to the out folder         $dstdir = join-path $destinationpath ($_.fullname | split-path | split-path -leaf)         new-item -itemtype directory -force -path $dstdir | out-null         [system.io.compression.zipfileextensions]::extracttofile($_, (join-path $dstdir $_.name), $true)     }     $zip.dispose() }     scriptblock id: f4c27d19-3ac2-45b9-9a9e-cc8eab9a461f  path: c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-fetchfromzip.ps1", "pid": 16024, "class": "ms_windows_event", "eventid": "4104", "version": "10.0.22631", "category": "execute a remote command", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "hostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "severity": "warning", "threadid": "36648", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "metaclass": "windows", "accountname": "us58432", "application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-fetchfromzip.ps1", "accountdomain": "corp", "rawmsghostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org"}, "source": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "summary": {"application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-fetchfromzip.ps1"}, "properties": {"class": "ms_windows_event", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "customer_id": "hexbdw276"}, "destination": "4104"}
description: this rule looks for a powershell command that includes the .net webclient class or any indication of a url. attackers can use this method when using powershell as a downloader for additional payload. this method could also be used for legitimate reasons. if this rule triggers an alert, inspect the url to be downloaded for evidence of malice. tune this rule by filtering on the "application", "filename", or "filepath" fields.

description: this rule looks for suspicious rundll32.exe process executions with command lines that invoke control_rundll or shellexec_rundll functions from shell32.dll, a core windows shell dll. attackers leverage this legitimate functionality as a living off the land binary (lolbin) to execute arbitrary code, launch dll payloads, executables, or even command-line arguments, serving as a versatile method for code execution and defense evasion.
message: windows methodology [rundll32 - shell32.dll arbitrary code execution]

description: powershell is a scripting language and command-line shell that is bundled with all modern versions of windows. while this tool is used for all kinds of legitimate purposes, attackers also leverage it to compromise windows systems. additionally, attackers frequently use obfuscation techniques in an attempt to bypass detection mechanisms. however, if obfuscation is detected, it can be a telltale sign of malicious intent. this rule detects a powershell argument that has signs of encoding obfuscation using the securestring object type.

description: injection vulnerabilities occur when an application sends untrusted data to an interpreter. this is a common attack vector for web applications. these flaws are often found with sql, ldap, xpath, or nosql queries; os commands; xml parsers, smtp headers, program arguments, etc. attackers commonly scan a web application and attempt to inject code to obtain unauthorized database information or to cause the application to perform in an unintended way which the attacker attempts to leverage. this rule looks for specific sql commands that are indicative of an injection attempt.
message: methodology - web app attack [injection union attempt]

description: this rule looks for suspicious rundll32.exe process executions with command lines that invoke control_rundll or shellexec_rundll functions from shell32.dll, a core windows shell dll. attackers leverage this legitimate functionality as a living off the land binary (lolbin) to execute arbitrary code, launch dll payloads, executables, or even command-line arguments, serving as a versatile method for code execution and defense evasion.
message: windows methodology [rundll32 - shell32.dll arbitrary code execution]

description: injection vulnerabilities occur when an application sends untrusted data to an interpreter. this is a common attack vector for web applications. these flaws are often found with sql, ldap, xpath, or nosql queries; os commands; xml parsers, smtp headers, program arguments, etc. attackers commonly scan a web application and attempt to inject code to obtain unauthorized database information or to cause the application to perform in an unintended way which the attacker attempts to leverage. this rule looks for specific sql commands that are indicative of an injection attempt.
message: methodology - web app attack [injection select attempt]

description: this rule looks for suspicious rundll32.exe process executions with command lines that invoke control_rundll or shellexec_rundll functions from shell32.dll, a core windows shell dll. attackers leverage this legitimate functionality as a living off the land binary (lolbin) to execute arbitrary code, launch dll payloads, executables, or even command-line arguments, serving as a versatile method for code execution and defense evasion.
message: windows methodology [rundll32 - shell32.dll arbitrary code execution]

description: psexec is a utility from microsoft that allows remote process execution on windows systems. while it can be used for legitimate uses, it has also been known to be used by threat actors when performing lateral movement. this rule simply looks for the presence of the psexec process name. it should be noted that any variations of the name of this process will not trigger this rule. the analyst should validate the legitimacy of the tool's existence or usage by checking with the system administrator or user.

alert_type_details: {"detail": {"msg": "creating scriptblock text (1 of 1):  function invoke-webrequestverifyhash ($url, $outfile, $hash) {     $success = $false     $null = @(         new-item -itemtype directory (split-path $outfile) -force | out-null         $ms = new-object io.memorystream         [net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol = ([net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls11 -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls12)         (new-object system.net.webclient).openread($url).copyto($ms)         $ms.seek(0, [system.io.seekorigin]::begin) | out-null         $actualhash = (get-filehash -inputstream $ms).hash         if ( $hash -eq $actualhash) {             $ms.seek(0, [system.io.seekorigin]::begin) | out-null             $filestream = new-object io.filestream $outfile, ([system.io.filemode]::create)             $ms.copyto($filestream);             $filestream.close()             $success = $true         }         else {             write-host -foregroundcolor red \"file hash mismatch, expected: $hash, actual: $actualhash\"         }     )     $success }     scriptblock id: 77630ae3-99c5-4908-a3af-ae532cfad688  path: c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1", "pid": 21412, "class": "ms_windows_event", "eventid": "4104", "version": "10.0.22631", "category": "execute a remote command", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "hostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "severity": "warning", "threadid": "40384", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "metaclass": "windows", "accountname": "us58432", "application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1", "accountdomain": "corp", "rawmsghostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org"}, "source": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "summary": {"application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1"}, "properties": {"class": "ms_windows_event", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "customer_id": "hexbdw276"}, "destination": "4104"}
description: this rule looks for a powershell command that includes the .net webclient class or any indication of a url. attackers can use this method when using powershell as a downloader for additional payload. this method could also be used for legitimate reasons. if this rule triggers an alert, inspect the url to be downloaded for evidence of malice. tune this rule by filtering on the "application", "filename", or "filepath" fields.

description: this rule looks for file downloads initiated by certutil based on the presence of command line arguments. certutil is a built-in windows tool that can be used to download arbitrary files from the command line. attackers have been known to use certutil to download malicious payloads to compromised systems. certutil is also used for legitimate purposes, so this rule may require tuning if it triggers an alert on legitimate web traffic.

description: this rule looks for suspicious use of schtasks.exe, a built-in windows utility. it flags when schtasks.exe creates, modifies, deletes, queries, or runs scheduled tasks that involve common living-off-the-land binaries (lolbins) or directly reference network activity (urls). attackers frequently leverage this for persistence, arbitrary code execution, or lateral movement. analysts should validate and tune this rule accordingly.

description: this rule looks for a windows task creation that includes a path containing "appdata". some malware is known to persist using this technique. if this rule triggers an alert, inspect the task created, including the script/binary configured to run for signs of malicious intent.

description: this rule looks for a command with a long string of consecutive base64 characters. this rule merely matches on characters and does not attempt to decode, so it could trigger on a string that is not base64-encoded. malware authors and windows attack tools will often encode long strings of commands in base64, with powershell directly supporting encoded commands. this could indicate malicious activity.

corp   logon id:  0x3aece320    target subject:   security id:  s-1-0-0   account name:  -   account domain:  -   logon id:  0x0    process information:   new process id:  0x4e6c   new process name: c:\\windows\\system32\\windowspowershell\\v1.0\\powershell.exe   token elevation type: tokenelevationtypefull (2)   mandatory label:  s-1-16-12288   creator process id: 0x6c34   creator process name: c:\\windows\\system32\\windowspowershell\\v1.0\\powershell.exe   process command line: \"powershell.exe\" & {write-host 1.downloading keyscrambler from official website to temp directory invoke-webrequest -uri \\\"\"https://download.qfxsoftware.com/download/latest/keyscrambler_setup.exe\\\"\" -outfile $env:temp\\keyscrambler_setup.exe write-host 2.installing keyscrambler with keyscrambler_setup.exe from temp directory start-process -filepath $env:temp\\keyscrambler_setup.exe -argumentlist /s -wait write-host 3.copying keyscrambler.exe to temp folder,to avoid permission issues, which calls keyscramblerie.dll in cwd i.e. temp copy-item \\\"\"c:\\program files (x86)\\keyscrambler\\keyscrambler.exe\\\"\" -destination $env:temp\\keyscrambler.exe write-host 4.executing keyscrambler.exe, you should see a popup of missing keyscramblerie.dll, you can close this popup start-process -filepath $env:temp\\keyscrambler.exe write-host 5.a modified keyscramblerie.dll can be copied to temp, which can be misused by attacker}    token elevation type indicates the type of token that was assigned to the new process in accordance with user account control policy.    type 1 is a full token with no privileges removed or groups disabled.  a full token is only used if user account control is disabled or if the user is the built-in administrator account or a service account.    type 2 is an elevated token with no privileges removed or groups disabled.  an elevated token is used when user account control is enabled and the user chooses to start the program using run as administrator.  an elevated token is also used when an application is configured to always require administrative privilege or to always require maximum privilege, and the user is a member of the administrators group.    type 3 is a limited token with administrative privileges removed and administrative groups disabled.  the limited token is used when user account control is enabled, the application does not require administrative privilege, and the user does not choose to start the program using run as administrator.", "pid": 20076, "args": "\"powershell.exe\" & {Write-Host $env:Temp\\KeyScrambler_Setup.exe Write-Host 2.Installing KeyScrambler with KeyScrambler_Setup.exe from temp directory Start-Process -FilePath $env:Temp\\KeyScrambler_Setup.exe -ArgumentList /S -Wait Write-Host 3.Copying KeyScrambler.exe to temp folder,to avoid permission issues, which calls KeyScramblerIE.dll in CWD i.e. temp Copy-Item \\\"\"C:\\Program Files (x86)\\KeyScrambler\\KeyScrambler.exe\\\"\" -Destination $env:TEMP\\KeyScrambler.exe Write-Host 4.Executing KeyScrambler.exe, you should see a popup of missing KeyScramblerIE.dll, you can close this description: this rule looks for a powershell command that includes the .net webclient class or any indication of a url. attackers can use this method when using powershell as a downloader for additional payload. this method could also be used for legitimate reasons. if this rule triggers an alert, inspect the url to be downloaded for evidence of malice. tune this rule by excluding benign commands in the 'args' or 'info' fields.

alert_type_details: {"detail": {"msg": "creating scriptblock text (1 of 1):  function invoke-webrequestverifyhash ($url, $outfile, $hash) {     $success = $false     $null = @(         new-item -itemtype directory (split-path $outfile) -force | out-null         $ms = new-object io.memorystream         [net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol = ([net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls11 -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls12)         (new-object system.net.webclient).openread($url).copyto($ms)         $ms.seek(0, [system.io.seekorigin]::begin) | out-null         $actualhash = (get-filehash -inputstream $ms).hash         if ( $hash -eq $actualhash) {             $ms.seek(0, [system.io.seekorigin]::begin) | out-null             $filestream = new-object io.filestream $outfile, ([system.io.filemode]::create)             $ms.copyto($filestream);             $filestream.close()             $success = $true         }         else {             write-host -foregroundcolor red \"file hash mismatch, expected: $hash, actual: $actualhash\"         }     )     $success }     scriptblock id: 663aaa38-2ff9-48f2-ac26-9b4fc898ca89  path: c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1", "pid": 9460, "class": "ms_windows_event", "eventid": "4104", "version": "10.0.22631", "category": "execute a remote command", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "hostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "severity": "warning", "threadid": "27140", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "metaclass": "windows", "accountname": "us58432", "application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1", "accountdomain": "corp", "rawmsghostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org"}, "source": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "summary": {"application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1"}, "properties": {"class": "ms_windows_event", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "customer_id": "hexbdw276"}, "destination": "4104"}

alert_type_details: {"detail": {"msg": "creating scriptblock text (1 of 1):  function invoke-fetchfromzip {     param(         [parameter(mandatory = $true, position = 0)]         [string]         $zipurl,         [parameter(mandatory = $true, position = 1)]         [string]         $targetfilter, # files that match this filter will be copied to the destinationpath, retaining their folder path from the zip         [parameter(mandatory = $true, position = 2)]         [string]         $destinationpath     )      # load zip methods     add-type -assemblyname system.io.compression.filesystem     [system.reflection.assembly]::loadwithpartialname('system.io.compression') | out-null      # read zip archive into memory     $ms = new-object io.memorystream     [net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol = ([net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls11 -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls12)     (new-object system.net.webclient).openread($zipurl).copyto($ms)     $zip = new-object system.io.compression.ziparchive($ms)      # ensure the output folder exists     $parent = split-path $destinationpath     $exists = test-path -path $parent     if ($exists -eq $false) {         $null = new-item -path $destinationpath -itemtype directory -force     }      # find all files in zip that match the filter (i.e. file extension)     $zip.entries |     where-object {             ($_.fullname -like $targetfilter)     } |     foreach-object {         # extract the selected items from the zip archive         # and copy them to the out folder         $dstdir = join-path $destinationpath ($_.fullname | split-path | split-path -leaf)         new-item -itemtype directory -force -path $dstdir | out-null         [system.io.compression.zipfileextensions]::extracttofile($_, (join-path $dstdir $_.name), $true)     }     $zip.dispose() }     scriptblock id: 30924d63-7167-49fa-a82b-ec8ca5e3ed90  path: c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-fetchfromzip.ps1", "pid": 29572, "class": "ms_windows_event", "eventid": "4104", "version": "10.0.22631", "category": "execute a remote command", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "hostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "severity": "warning", "threadid": "4492", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "metaclass": "windows", "accountname": "us58432", "application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-fetchfromzip.ps1", "accountdomain": "corp", "rawmsghostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org"}, "source": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "summary": {"application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-fetchfromzip.ps1"}, "properties": {"class": "ms_windows_event", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "customer_id": "hexbdw276"}, "destination": "4104"}

version number of this module.      moduleversion     = '2.1.0'        # id used to uniquely identify this module      guid              = '8f492621-18f8-432e-9532-b1d54d3e90bd'        # author of this module      author            = 'casey smith @subtee, josh rickard @msadministrator, carrie roberts @oroneequalsone, matt graeber @mattifestation'        # description of the functionality provided by this module      description       = 'a powershell module that runs atomic red team tests from yaml definition files.'        # minimum version of the windows powershell engine required by this module      powershellversion = '5.0'        # modules that must be imported into the global environment prior to importing this module      requiredmodules   = @('powershell-yaml')        # script files (.ps1) that are run in the caller's environment prior to importing this module.      # atomicclassschema.ps1 needs to be present in the caller's scope in order for the built-in classes to surface properly.      scriptstoprocess  = @('private\\atomicclassschema.ps1', 'public\\config.ps1')        # functions to export from this module, for best performance, do not use wildcards and do not delete the entry, use an empty array if there are no functions to export.      functionstoexport = @(          'invoke-atomictest',          'get-atomictechnique',          'new-atomictechnique',          'new-atomictest',          'new-atomictestinputargument',          'new-atomictestdependency',          'start-atomicgui',          'stop-atomicgui',          'invoke-setupatomicrunner',          'invoke-generatenewschedule',          'invoke-refreshexistingschedule',          'invoke-atomicrunner',          'get-schedule',          'invoke-kickoffatomicrunner',          'get-preferredipaddress',          'invoke-killprocesstree'      )        # variables to export from this module      variablestoexport = '*'        nestedmodules     = @(          \"public\\default-executionlogger.psm1\",          \"public\\attire-executionlogger.psm1\",          \"public\\syslog-executionlogger.psm1\",          \"public\\winevent-executionlogger.psm1\"      )        # private data to pass to the module specified in rootmodule/moduletoprocess. this may also contain a psdata hashtable with additional module metadata used by powershell.      privatedata       = @{            psdata = @{                # tags applied to this module. these help with module discovery in online galleries.              tags         = @('security', 'defense')                # a url to the license for this module.              licenseuri   = 'https://github.com/redcanaryco/invoke-atomicredteam/blob/master/license.txt'                # a url to the main website for this project.              projecturi   = 'https://github.com/redcanaryco/invoke-atomicredteam'                # a url to an icon representing this module.              # iconuri = ''                # releasenotes of this module              releasenotes = @'  1.0.2  -----  * add support for custom execution loggers    1.0.1  -----  * adding 'powershell-yaml' to requiredmodules in the module manifest    1.0.0  -----  * initial release for submission to the powershell gallery  '@            } # description: teamcity is a continuous integration/continuous deployment (ci/cd) application used by organizations for devops and other software development activities. a critical vulnerability, reported as cve-2023-3267, that could bypass the authorization was found on this jetbrains teamcity server. successful exploitation of this vulnerability may allow an unauthenticated attacker to execute the arbitrary code on the server. this rule looks for the http post requests, what appears to be unsuccessful exploitation attempts of this vulnerability. more information on this vulnerability can be found in the infolinks given.

description: this rule looks for suspicious rundll32.exe process executions with command lines that invoke control_rundll or shellexec_rundll functions from shell32.dll, a core windows shell dll. attackers leverage this legitimate functionality as a living off the land binary (lolbin) to execute arbitrary code, launch dll payloads, executables, or even command-line arguments, serving as a versatile method for code execution and defense evasion.

type indicates the type of token that was assigned to the new process in accordance with user account control policy.    type 1 is a full token with no privileges removed or groups disabled.  a full token is only used if user account control is disabled or if the user is the built-in administrator account or a service account.    type 2 is an elevated token with no privileges removed or groups disabled.  an elevated token is used when user account control is enabled and the user chooses to start the program using run as administrator.  an elevated token is also used when an application is configured to always require administrative privilege or to always require maximum privilege, and the user is a member of the administrators group.    type 3 is a limited token with administrative alert_type_details: {"detail": {"msg": "creating scriptblock text (1 of 1):  function invoke-webrequestverifyhash ($url, $outfile, $hash) {     $success = $false     $null = @(         new-item -itemtype directory (split-path $outfile) -force | out-null         $ms = new-object io.memorystream         [net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol = ([net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls11 -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls12)         (new-object system.net.webclient).openread($url).copyto($ms)         $ms.seek(0, [system.io.seekorigin]::begin) | out-null         $actualhash = (get-filehash -inputstream $ms).hash         if ( $hash -eq $actualhash) {             $ms.seek(0, [system.io.seekorigin]::begin) | out-null             $filestream = new-object io.filestream $outfile, ([system.io.filemode]::create)             $ms.copyto($filestream);             $filestream.close()             $success = $true         }         else {             write-host -foregroundcolor red \"file hash mismatch, expected: $hash, actual: $actualhash\"         }     )     $success }     scriptblock id: c49f4e6c-7817-4979-85da-1348c954a8b8  path: c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1", "pid": 22260, "class": "ms_windows_event", "eventid": "4104", "version": "10.0.22631", "category": "execute a remote command", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "hostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "severity": "warning", "threadid": "24736", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "metaclass": "windows", "accountname": "us58432", "application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1", "accountdomain": "corp", "rawmsghostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org"}, "source": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "summary": {"application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1"}, "properties": {"class": "ms_windows_event", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "customer_id": "hexbdw276"}, "destination": "4104"}
description: this rule looks for a powershell command that includes the .net webclient class or any indication of a url. attackers can use this method when using powershell as a downloader for additional payload. this method could also be used for legitimate reasons. if this rule triggers an alert, inspect the url to be downloaded for evidence of malice. tune this rule by filtering on the "application", "filename", or "filepath" fields.

description: psexec is a utility from microsoft that allows remote process execution on windows systems. while it can be used for legitimate uses, it has also been known to be used by threat actors when performing lateral movement. this rule simply looks for the presence of the psexec process name. it should be noted that any variations of the name of this process will not trigger this rule. the analyst should validate the legitimacy of the tool's existence or usage by checking with the system administrator or user.

description: this rule looks for suspicious rundll32.exe process executions with command lines that invoke control_rundll or shellexec_rundll functions from shell32.dll, a core windows shell dll. attackers leverage this legitimate functionality as a living off the land binary (lolbin) to execute arbitrary code, launch dll payloads, executables, or even command-line arguments, serving as a versatile method for code execution and defense evasion.
message: windows methodology [rundll32 - shell32.dll arbitrary code execution]

description: a critical elevation of privilege vulnerability, reported as cve-2023-29357, was found in microsoft sharepoint server. exploitation of this vulnerability would allow remote attackers to gain elevated privileges and enable them to perform malicous activities remotely. this rule looks for 'get' requests that was observed in the exploitation of this vulnerability.

description: this rule looks for the creation of a windows scheduled task from the command line. attackers use this feature as a persistence mechanism or to invoke a malicious job or script at a given time. these tasks are often named as simply as 1.exe or as innocuously as defrag.bat. if this rule triggers an alert, check whether the scheduled task contains malicious activity.
message: windows methodology [scheduled task creation from cli]

description: a malware callback alert detects network activity that matches a malware command and control (c2) signature. this should only fire when a host is already compromised. the recommended action is to investigate the host and possibly remove it from the network. this activity will be blocked if the nx is in block mode.

policy.    type 1 is a full token with no privileges removed or groups disabled.  a full token is only used if user account control is disabled or if the user is the built-in administrator account or description: this rule looks for the certutil windows built-in command being executed with a suspicious decode argument. suspicious indicators include decoding to or from the "appdata" directory and the presence of a dll file extension. this could indicate that malicious payload was transferred as disguised encoded text and converted to its final payload on the host. if argument logging is enabled, the final argument of this command should indicate the final decoded file.

description: microsoft windows contains accessibility features that can be launched with a key combination before a user has logged in (ex: when the user is on the windows logon screen). two most common accessibility programs are c:\windows\system32\sethc.exe, launched when the shift key is pressed five times and c:\windows\system32\utilman.exe, launched when the windows + u key combination is pressed. adversaries can modify the way these programs are launched to get a command prompt or backdoor without logging in to the system. common methods used by adversaries include replacing accessibility feature binaries with "cmd.exe" (or another program that provides backdoor access). subsequently, pressing the appropriate key combination at the login screen while sitting at the keyboard or when connected over remote desktop protocol will cause the replaced file to be executed with system privileges. this rule detects windows process execution event where argument field contains one of these binaries and "cmd.exe".

description: this rule looks for suspicious rundll32.exe process executions with command lines that invoke control_rundll or shellexec_rundll functions from shell32.dll, a core windows shell dll. attackers leverage this legitimate functionality as a living off the land binary (lolbin) to execute arbitrary code, launch dll payloads, executables, or even command-line arguments, serving as a versatile method for code execution and defense evasion.

description: the windows command "ping -n x" can be indicative of nefarious activity performed by actors or malware trying to move stealthily through an environment or to map a network. it is also sometimes used by legitimate software conducting connectivity checks. if this alerts the analyst should determine the underlying cause of the activity. additionally, if it is found to be malicious activity the security logs should be reviewed for the contacted system to be sure no lateral movement was successful. this rule requires command line argument logging, such as from sysmon.

that was assigned to the new process in accordance with user account control policy.    type 1 is a full token with no privileges removed or groups disabled.  a full token is only used if user account description: this rule looks for a bits admin command with the "/transfer" argument. this command can be used to instruct bits to download a file. bits is a built-in windows tool that can be used to download arbitrary files. attackers have been known to use this to download additional payloads to compromised systems. bits is also used for legitimate purposes. however, seeing a one-time bits download in this way may be suspicious enough to warrant additional investigation.

description: attackers moving laterally often times use built-in windows commands. one commonly used command is "net". this command can be used to enumerate local or domain users, groups, computers, etc. it can also be used to add, remove and make changes to users and groups. this rule detects a user being added to a local or domain group which could be an indicator of nefarious activity.

description: this rule looks for the initiation of a windows scheduled task from the command line. attackers use this feature as a persistence mechanism, or to invoke a malicious job or script at a given time. these tasks are often named as simply as 1.exe or as innocuously as defrag.bat. determine whether the scheduled task contains malicious activity.
message: windows methodology [scheduled task initiation from cli]

description: teamcity is a continuous integration/continuous deployment (ci/cd) application used by organizations for devops and other software development activities. a critical vulnerability, reported as cve-2023-3267, that could bypass the authorization was found on this jetbrains teamcity server. successful exploitation of this vulnerability may allow an unauthenticated attacker to execute the arbitrary code on the server. this rule looks for the http post requests, what appears to be unsuccessful exploitation attempts of this vulnerability. more information on this vulnerability can be found in the infolinks given.

A critical vulnerability, reported as CVE-2023-3267, that could bypass the authorization was found on this JetBrains TeamCity server. Successful exploitation of this vulnerability may allow an unauthenticated attacker to execute the arbitrary code on the server. This rule looks for the HTTP POST requests, what appears to be unsuccessful exploitation attempts of this vulnerability. closed_reason:
new server 2025 vm.  we've seen this a bunch recently (see attached).  
looks like c:\windows\system32\securityhealthservice.exe is setting machine\system\controlset001\control\lsa\runasppl = 0
description: the runasppl registry key can be used to establish lsa protection i.e. prevent dumping of lsass process. this ioc detects disabling of lsa protection via this registry key. this is associated with mitre att&ck (r) tactic(s): defense evasion, defense evasion and technique(s): t1112, t1003.001.

description: this rule looks for adrecon tool/commandlines which extracts and combines various artefacts out of an ad environment. adversaries may use these tools to create a backup of an active directory database for password cracking or password hash reuse. these tools may also be leveraged as a means to perform post-exploitation reconnaissance/discover activities on a windows domain. if this rule is triggered, inspect other commands ran by the same user for further indicators of nefarious activity.

alert_type_details: {"detail": {"msg": "creating scriptblock text (1 of 1):  function invoke-webrequestverifyhash ($url, $outfile, $hash) {     $success = $false     $null = @(         new-item -itemtype directory (split-path $outfile) -force | out-null         $ms = new-object io.memorystream         [net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol = ([net.servicepointmanager]::securityprotocol -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls11 -bor [net.securityprotocoltype]::tls12)         (new-object system.net.webclient).openread($url).copyto($ms)         $ms.seek(0, [system.io.seekorigin]::begin) | out-null         $actualhash = (get-filehash -inputstream $ms).hash         if ( $hash -eq $actualhash) {             $ms.seek(0, [system.io.seekorigin]::begin) | out-null             $filestream = new-object io.filestream $outfile, ([system.io.filemode]::create)             $ms.copyto($filestream);             $filestream.close()             $success = $true         }         else {             write-host -foregroundcolor red \"file hash mismatch, expected: $hash, actual: $actualhash\"         }     )     $success }     scriptblock id: a4731fe2-a35c-4816-a18d-5acb60a9fcfb  path: c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1", "pid": 35932, "class": "ms_windows_event", "eventid": "4104", "version": "10.0.22631", "category": "execute a remote command", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "hostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "severity": "warning", "threadid": "28400", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "metaclass": "windows", "accountname": "us58432", "application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1", "accountdomain": "corp", "rawmsghostname": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org"}, "source": "a43911-m.corp.premera.org", "summary": {"application": "c:\\atomicredteam\\invoke-atomicredteam\\public\\invoke-webrequestverifyhash.ps1"}, "properties": {"class": "ms_windows_event", "deviceid": "8652095f7abc", "meta_cbid": 888888888, "customer_id": "hexbdw276"}, "destination": "4104"}
description: this rule looks for a powershell command that includes the .net webclient class or any indication of a url. attackers can use this method when using powershell as a downloader for additional payload. this method could also be used for legitimate reasons. if this rule triggers an alert, inspect the url to be downloaded for evidence of malice. tune this rule by filtering on the "application", "filename", or "filepath" fields.

elevation type indicates the type of token that was assigned to the new process in accordance with user account control policy.    type 1 is a full token with no privileges removed or groups disabled. groups disabled.  an elevated token is used when user account control is enabled and the user chooses to start the program using run as administrator.  an elevated token is also used when an application is configured to always require administrative privilege or to always require maximum privilege, and the user is a member of the administrators group.    type 3 is a limited token with description: the rule looks for the execution of the curl process on windows systems, specifically targeting the instances where curl is executed with command-line arguments that exhibit behavior that could potentially involve storing of the obtained output to a local file. adversaries can use curl to download publicly available information from legitimate websites during the reconnaissance phase of an attack. this information could be used to identify vulnerabilities in a system or gather details about a target network. the analyst should verify the legitimacy of this behavior and should use this as a starting point to understand if further investigation is necessary.

disabled.  a full token is only used if user account control is disabled or if the user is the built-in administrator account or a service account.    type 2 is an elevated token with no privileges removed or groups disabled.  an elevated token is used when user account control is enabled and the user chooses to start the program using run as administrator.  an elevated token is also used when an application is configured to always require administrative privilege or to always require maximum privilege, and the user is a member of the administrators group.    type 3 is a limited token with administrative privileges removed and administrative groups disabled.  the limited token is used when user account control is enabled, the application does not require administrative privilege, and description: certutil.exe is a native windows binary that is part of the built-in certificate services. it has been used by attackers to natively encoded/decode base64 data. an executable that has been base64 encoded will start with the string tvq. this alert also looks for file write events containing the certificate header and the base64 encoded mz header . this is associated with mitre att&ck (r) tactic(s): defense evasion and technique(s): t1140.

description: this rule looks for a powershell command that includes the .net webclient class or any indication of a url. attackers can use this method when using powershell as a downloader for additional payload. this method could also be used for legitimate reasons. if this rule triggers an alert, inspect the url to be downloaded for evidence of malice. tune this rule by filtering on the "application", "filename", or "filepath" fields.

description: this rule looks for the initiation of a windows scheduled task from the command line. attackers use this feature as a persistence mechanism, or to invoke a malicious job or script at a given time. these tasks are often named as simply as 1.exe or as innocuously as defrag.bat. determine whether the scheduled task contains malicious activity.
message: windows methodology [scheduled task initiation from cli]

description: this rule looks for suspicious rundll32.exe process executions with command lines that invoke control_rundll or shellexec_rundll functions from shell32.dll, a core windows shell dll. attackers leverage this legitimate functionality as a living off the land binary (lolbin) to execute arbitrary code, launch dll payloads, executables, or even command-line arguments, serving as a versatile method for code execution and defense evasion.
message: windows methodology [rundll32 - shell32.dll arbitrary code execution]

description: this rule looks for the initiation of a windows scheduled task from the command line. attackers use this feature as a persistence mechanism, or to invoke a malicious job or script at a given time. these tasks are often named as simply as 1.exe or as innocuously as defrag.bat. determine whether the scheduled task contains malicious activity.
message: windows methodology [scheduled task initiation from cli]

description: this ioc covers powershell command line arguments commonly used by attackers to hide activity and avoid dropping malware to disk.   these commands could be used by administrators for legitimate operations, but are more often observed in malicious operations.  if there is no known authorized use in your environment, investigate the source endpoint and any endpoints named in the command line arguments for further evidence.  malicious command line arguments will often be heavily obfuscated or encoded. this is associated with mitre att&ck (r) tactic(s): execution and technique(s): t1059.001.

description: psexec is a utility from microsoft that allows remote process execution on windows systems. while it can be used for legitimate uses, it has also been known to be used by threat actors when performing lateral movement. this rule simply looks for the presence of the psexec service name. it should be noted that any variations of the name of this service will not trigger this rule. the analyst should validate the legitimacy of the tool's existence or usage by checking with the system administrator or user.

description: this rule looks for suspicious rundll32.exe process executions with command lines that invoke control_rundll or shellexec_rundll functions from shell32.dll, a core windows shell dll. attackers leverage this legitimate functionality as a living off the land binary (lolbin) to execute arbitrary code, launch dll payloads, executables, or even command-line arguments, serving as a versatile method for code execution and defense evasion.
message: windows methodology [rundll32 - shell32.dll arbitrary code execution]

description: mimikatz is a pentesting tool for testing windows password security. this tool has also been known to be used by malicious actors for stealing windows credentials. this rule detects a number of command line arguments that could be an indicator of mimikatz usage. this may be useful when the binary or script used to invoke mimikatz has been renamed.

elevated token with no privileges removed or groups disabled.  an elevated token is used when user account control is enabled and the user chooses to start the program using run as administrator.  an elevated token is also used when an application is configured to always require administrative privilege or to always require maximum privilege, and the user is a member of the administrators group.    type 3 is a limited token with administrative privileges removed and administrative groups disabled.  the limited token is used when user account control is enabled, the application does not require alert_type_details: {"detail": {"msg": "creating scriptblock text (1 of 1):  @{        # script module or binary module file associated with this manifest.      rootmodule        = 'invoke-atomicredteam.psm1'        # version number of this module.      moduleversion     = '2.1.0'        # id used to uniquely identify this module      guid              = '8f492621-18f8-432e-9532-b1d54d3e90bd'        # author of this module      author            = 'casey smith @subtee, josh rickard @msadministrator, carrie roberts @oroneequalsone, matt graeber @mattifestation'        # company or vendor of this module      companyname       = 'red canary, inc.'        # copyright statement for this module      copyright         = '(c) 2021 red canary. all rights reserved.'        # description of the functionality provided by this module      description       = 'a powershell module that runs atomic red team tests from yaml definition files.'        # minimum version of the windows powershell engine required by this module      powershellversion = '5.0'        # modules that must be imported into the global environment prior to importing this module      requiredmodules   = @('powershell-yaml')        # script files (.ps1) that are run in the caller's environment prior to importing this module.      # atomicclassschema.ps1 needs to be present in the caller's scope in order for the built-in classes to surface properly.      scriptstoprocess  = @('private\\atomicclassschema.ps1', 'public\\config.ps1')        # functions to export from this module, for best performance, do not use wildcards and do not delete the entry, use an empty array if there are no functions to export.      functionstoexport = @(          'invoke-atomictest',          'get-atomictechnique',          'new-atomictechnique',          'new-atomictest',          'new-atomictestinputargument',          'new-atomictestdependency',          'start-atomicgui',          'stop-atomicgui',          'invoke-setupatomicrunner',          'invoke-generatenewschedule',          'invoke-refreshexistingschedule',          'invoke-atomicrunner',          'get-schedule',          'invoke-kickoffatomicrunner',          'get-preferredipaddress',          'invoke-killprocesstree'      )        # variables to export from this module      variablestoexport = '*'        nestedmodules     = @(          \"public\\default-executionlogger.psm1\",          \"public\\attire-executionlogger.psm1\",          \"public\\syslog-executionlogger.psm1\",          \"public\\winevent-executionlogger.psm1\"      )        # private data to pass to the module specified in rootmodule/moduletoprocess. this may also contain a psdata hashtable with additional module metadata used by powershell.      privatedata       = @{            psdata = @{                # tags applied to this module. these help with module discovery in online galleries.              tags         = @('security', 'defense')                # a url to the license for this module.              licenseuri   = 'https://github.com/redcanaryco/invoke-atomicredteam/blob/master/license.txt'                # a url to the main website for this project.              projecturi   = 'https://github.com/redcanaryco/invoke-atomicredteam'                # a url to an icon representing this module.              # iconuri = ''                # releasenotes of this module              releasenotes = @'  1.0.2  -----  * add support for custom execution loggers    1.0.1  -----  * adding 'powershell-yaml' to requiredmodules in the module manifest    1.0.0  -----  * initial release for submission to the powershell gallery  '@            } # description: certutil.exe is a native windows binary that is part of the built-in certificate services. it has been used by attackers to natively encoded/decode base64 data. however, on 2017-07-23 researcher casey smith (@subtee) discovered that certutil.exe can be used as a memory-only downloader via the built-in -ping argument. this ioc looks for this -ping or -urlcache arguments in certutil.exe. the resultant payload is usually written to disk or filtered (via find, findstr, -split, etc.) and then piped into another binary like cmd.exe, powershell.exe, etc. this is associated with mitre att&ck (r) tactic(s): command and control and technique(s): t1105.
message: trellix endpoint hx [ioc alert - certutil.exe downloader a (utility)]
